Get Quickly to the Bottom of the Issues and Increase Your Chances of Getting Your Case Dismissed, Charges or Fine Reduced. When the Department of Justice (DOJ), Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) show up at your door, they are looking for something that could possibly connect you to a federal fraud crime or sole allegation where you or your company can be exposed to civil or criminal liability. The go-to allegations are either False Claims Act violations, wire fraud, or conspiracy to commit fraud. In either situation, knowing how to respond is critical; knowing how to respond to civil investigative demands (CIDs) or subpoenas is just as important. As a government contractor, your financial health and reputation are at risk when facing a federal civil or criminal investigation. Watson & Associates’ law firm has compiled a sophisticated government contracting and government procurement fraud practice of skilled federal lawyers—many of whom are former government contract officials or former DOJ prosecutors. The law firm helps companies of all sizes manage federal investigations, indictments, and trials involving federal contracts. When it comes to federal procurement law for contractors, we help you avoid costly legal mistakes commonly seen in the federal procurement industry. With offices in Washington, DC; Denver, Colorado; Houston, Texas; Miami, Florida; and Los Angeles, California, the government contract fraud lawyers at Watson & Associates, LLC represent federal contractors nationwide and internationally, including small businesses and large defense contractors. Our clients include private security contractors, logistics companies, intelligence and engineering services firms, information technology (IT Services) providers, construction contractors, various service contractors, and medical support companies. Our services include: Contract non-compliance investigations. Regulatory non-compliance investigations. Small business and SBA investigations. Procurement fraud investigations. Defective pricing investigations. Suspension and debarment actions. False Claims Act (FCA) investigations (civil and criminal). Congressional investigations. Civil investigative demands and subpoenas Trade Agreements Act and Buy American Act fraud cases High-profile cases involving corporate CEOs and management. Companies that seek a cheap way out quickly learn that hiring traditional criminal defense lawyers with no experience in federal government contracting can be an expensive disaster. At Watson & Associates, LLC, our small-business and large-defense-contractor clients understand the value of forming a 4-6-member legal team to quickly identify the underlying issues and seek to have charges reduced or dismissed. This is our law firm’s number one priority. For Federal Contractors, a Single Misstep Can End It All When your business works with the federal government, the opportunities can be extraordinary. But so are the risks. What may seem like a minor compliance oversight today could become the center of a federal investigation tomorrow. And in Washington, D.C.—where agencies like the DOJ, SBA, and OIG are ramping up enforcement—you need more than a lawyer. You need a top government contracts attorney with a planned strategy. Our government contracts attorneys represent companies and executives nationwide, but our practice is deeply rooted in Washington, D.C. , where most federal procurement investigations begin. We’re trusted by clients who understand that defending against allegations isn’t about being aggressive—it’s about being strategic, deliberate, and prepared to protect what’s most important: your business, your freedom, and your reputation. Main Content: FAR Termination for Default Lawyers & FAR Termination for Convenience Attorneys Washington DC 2024 Skip to content Termination for Default (T4D): What Federal Contractors Need to Understand Before Deciding Whether to Fight A termination for default changes how the government sees your company. It is not just the end of a contract. It is a formal statement that the government believes your business failed to perform—and that belief follows you. For contractors with meaningful federal portfolios, a T4D raises questions that extend well beyond the project just terminated for default. It affects past performance evaluations, future awards, bonding capacity, internal morale, and how competitors position themselves against you. And yet, many FAR termination for default decisions are made under pressure, with incomplete facts, or without proper consideration of the Federal Acquisition Regulation. That disconnect is where experienced contractors begin to ask a different question—not “How do we move on?” but “Is this defensible?” But you wouldn’t know that—unless you’ve been on the other side of the table. Termination for Default Meaning — What the Government Is Really Saying The termination for default meaning is often misunderstood. When a contracting officer issues a termination for default, the government is asserting that the contractor failed to perform in a material way. This could involve alleged schedule delays, quality issues, staffing concerns, or failures to meet contract specifications. But a T4D is not a judicial finding. It is an administrative decision. That distinction matters. Being terminated for default does not mean the government is correct. It means the government has taken a position—one that must be supported by facts, procedure, and compliance with the FAR. If those elements are missing, the termination for default may be improper, premature, or legally unsustainable. Why Termination for Default Happens More Often Than Contractors Expect Large contractors and small businesses alike are terminated for default more frequently than many expect, especially in high-pressure environments involving schedule acceleration, scope changes, supply chain disruptions, or agency-driven delays. From the government’s perspective, a T4D can serve multiple purposes. It shifts risk. It creates leverage. And, in some cases, it tests whether the contractor has the resources, resolve, and sophistication to challenge the decision. As former contracting officials, we understand this dynamic. We know that not every termination for default reflects a careful weighing of the record. Sometimes it reflects urgency. Sometimes optics. Sometimes, there is internal agency pressure. Understanding that reality is critical before deciding whether to accept a termination for default or push back. This is where our Washington DC termination for default lawyers can help. Our Perspective: Former Contracting Officials Who Know the Playbook We’ve worked inside government agencies. Some of our termination for default attorneys sat where that CO now sits. We’ve issued FAR terminations—and we’ve overturned them. And we know the truth: A government contract T4D is as much a pressure test as it is a decision. It forces the contractor to ask: Do I have the resources to respond? Is it worth challenging? Will they back down if I fight? What is Debarment of Government Contractors or Employees? What is debarment? Debarment of government contractors refers to the action taken by a government agency or entity to exclude a contractor or individual from participating in government contracts or procurement activities. It is a serious consequence resulting from violations, misconduct, or non-compliance with applicable laws, regulations, or contractual obligations. Being debarred from government contracts should not be a penalty or retaliation to a contractor or emplee. Instead, it should be a means of protecting the government’s interests by ensuring that contractors with a history of wrongdoing or non-compliance are not awarded future contracts. The primary purpose of debarment is to maintain the integrity of the government contracting process and safeguard taxpayer funds. When a contractor is debarred under FAR 9.4 , they are temporarily or permanently prohibited from bidding on or being awarded government contracts, subcontracts, grants, loans, or other federal assistance. This exclusion applies to all government agencies and may extend to affiliated companies or individuals associated with the debarred contractor. The debarment process typically involves an investigation and determination of wrongdoing by the relevant government agency. The agency will provide the contractor with notice of the proposed debarment and an opportunity to respond, present evidence, or seek a hearing to challenge the proposed action. The grounds for debarment under FAR 9.4 can vary but generally include serious violations such as fraud, bribery, antitrust violations, embezzlement, falsification of records, or other significant non-compliance with contractual or legal requirements. The duration of debarment can range from a few months to several years, depending on the severity of the misconduct and the contractor’s actions to remedy the issues. During the debarment period, contractors are expected to address the underlying causes of the misconduct, implement corrective actions, and demonstrate their commitment to compliance and ethical business practices. Reinstatement or lifting of the debarment can be considered if the contractor can prove that they have taken appropriate remedial measures and pose no further risk to the government’s interests. It’s important to note that debarment is distinct from suspension, which is a temporary action taken pending the outcome of an investigation or legal proceedings. While both suspension and debarment involve exclusion from government contracts, debarment typically has longer-term implications. Former DOJ Prosecutors and Procurement Officials on Our National Defense Team. Government contactors and healthcare companies can face serious consequences if charged and convicted for 31 USC 3729 federal False Claims Act violations. To stand a chance of prevailing during the investigative stages or post-indictment, CEOs must make a prompt decision on how best to defend the company. When you are a federal contractor, your chances of being indicted can be significantly high if you do not have federal False Claims Act criminal defense lawyers on your team who are not experienced in federal procurement laws. Federal prosecutors sometimes bring charges when there is no intent to defraud the government but also prevail because the defense attorney is also not familiar with the underlying statutes that you have allegedly violated. Examples include the Trade Agreements Act (TAA), Buy American Act (BAA), HUBZone, SDVOSB and FAR regulations. call 1.866.601.5518 and speak to practice leader, Theodore Watson. Government Contract Fraud Wire Fraud Conspiracy to Commit Fraud SBA small business fraud Healthcare fraud defense Trade Agreements Act fraud Buy American Act fraud SEC fraud Federal False Claims Act criminal defense What is the False Claims Act? The False Claims Act applies to companies and any individual that or indirectly contracts with the United States government. Individuals or businesses that knowingly submit or causes to be submitted a false or fraudulent claim with intent to secure payment or approval from a federal office or federal agency can be liable under 31 USC 3729 . The knowing submission of a false or fraudulent claim can be actual knowledge or your failure to conduct due diligence. If the prosecutor can prove that you should have known that it was billing the government improperly, this is enough to establish a False Claims Act violation. What Are False Claims Act Penalties? The False Claims Statute contains both civil and criminal penalties. A federal case can either be civil or criminal, or both. Civil FCA Liability : Triple damages and a penalty of up to $ 11,000 per claim if you knowingly submits or causes submission of a false or fraudulent claim to the US government. Criminal False Claims Liability (18 USC 287): Healthcare providers who intentionally present fraudulent claims to the government for reimbursement with the knowledge that such claims were false, fictitious, or fraudulent are exposed to five year imprisonment and a fine of $ 250,000 (individuals) or $ 500,000 (companies) for federal felony convictions and $ 100,000 (individuals) or $ 200,000 (companies) for misdemeanor convictions— for each claim. Watson & Associates, LLC federal False Claims Act lawyers have over 20 years of government contracting and procurement experience. In addition, we also have federal criminal defense lawyers who understand the False Claims Act and how federal prosecutors weave their way into FCA criminal charges. As criminal and civil FCA defense lawyers, we’re veterans of the government contracting marketplace. Our criminal and civil Federal Whistleblower Defense Lawyers understand government contract law (federal) and the issues that can lead to investigations, criminal indictments, and a jury conviction at trial. We also provide legal defense in Qui Tam and suspension and debarment cases. As federal False Claims Act investigation attorneys, we understand the unique ethical complexities and regulations that government contractors face. As federal False Claims Act criminal defense attorneys, we need work with top corporate decision-makers to investigate, plan, and execute a well-thought-out legal defense. Given the serious risks and reputation involved, we do not cut corners. However, our goal is to resolve cases before they reach trial. We provide nationwide legal defense from the investigation stage throughout trial. Call our federal False Claims Act criminal defense attorneys. Small businesses Large businesses Prime and subcontractors facing federal charges Buy American and Trade Agreements Act What’s Really Happening When Your Company Enters a Federal Investigation When the Department of Justice ( DOJ) or an Office of Inspector General initiates contact with your company regarding potential government contract fraud, they’ve typically been building their case for months—sometimes years—before you’re aware of i t. A whistleblower may have filed a qui tam complaint. A routine audit revealed irregularities. A contracting officer noticed patterns in your submissions. By the time your government contract fraud lawyer is engaged, the investigation is already sophisticated and well-documented. Here’s what actually happens: Under Federal Acquisition Regulation Part 3, contracting officers are legally mandated to refer suspected fraud to the DOJ or OIG. They cannot overlook it, even if the issue seems minor to them. Once that referral is made, federal prosecutors and investigators begin a systematic evidence-gathering process. They’ll examine your invoices line by line. They’ll interview your employees. They’ll subpoena your emails. They’re constructing a narrative about your company and your conduct. This is precisely when you need an experienced government contract fraud lawyer who understands both criminal defense and federal procurement law. They understand that government contract fraud lawyer representation often begins late in the process, so they’re designing their investigation to withstand legal challenge. By the time you reach out for help, the prosecutorial theory is already solid. This is precisely why your first call should be to an experienced government contract fraud lawyer before speaking to anyone else—before responding to documents, before employee interviews, before anything else. Your government contract fraud lawyer can intercept the investigation at critical junctures and prevent irreversible mistakes. Civil Investigative Demands: Understanding the Government’s Weapon A Civil Investigative Demand represents one of the most powerful pre-litigation tools available to federal prosecutors pursuing government contract fraud cases. Unlike traditional discovery in civil lawsuits, the DOJ can issue a CID without court approval, before filing any lawsuit, and with sweeping authority to demand documents, testimony, and information relevant to government contract fraud investigations. When you receive a CID , the government is signaling they have “reason to believe” you possess information relevant to their government contract fraud inquiry. T hese demands often request years of financial records, employee communications, contract files, and sworn testimony from your executives. You typically have 30 days to respond—though extensions can be negotiated by a skilled government contract fraud lawyer . But here’s what contractors frequently don’t understand: failing to respond or providing inadequate responses to a government contract fraud lawyer’s CID creates additional liability. The prosecutor can petition the court to compel compliance. More dangerously, your response itself becomes evidence—every document you produce, every explanation you provide can expand the government’s theory against you in government contract fraud prosecutions. A qualified government contract fraud lawyer knows that CID responses in government contract fraud matters require sophisticated strategy. You’re not simply gathering documents and mailing them back. You’re making calculated decisions about scope, privilege, and what information to produce in what order. Your government contract fraud lawyer will challenge overbroad requests, assert attorney-client privilege where applicable, and ensure your response doesn’t inadvertently create new investigative angles for prosecutors pursuing government contract fraud allegations . Defense Contractor Fraud Attorney Services Other Common types of cases that our government fraud lawyer s handle include: General Contractor Fraud False Claims Act Violations (FCA Claims – Civil and Criminal) Allegations of submitting false invoices, claims, or certifications to the government Bid rigging, kickbacks, contract manipulation, or competitive bidding violations Improper allocation of costs to government contracts, labor mischarging, or overhead violations Defective Pricing Cost Mischarging Overcharging the Government Bribing Government Officials Progress Payment Fraud Price Fixing Defective Products Kickback to Government Officials Billing for Services Not Performed Procurement Fraud Roofing Contractor Fraud Lawyer Buy American Act fraud and Trade Agreements Act Compliance Fraud SBA small business fraud (SDVOSB, 8(a) BD,and HUBZone certification fraud and size standard violations FAR non-compliance fraud disputes Mentor protege and pass-through schemes Target Letters A federal target letter means the U.S. Attorney’s Office believes you or your company committed a specific government contract fraud crime and you are now the named focus of a grand jury investigation. These letters typically identify the statutes prosecutors believe you violated—18 U.S.C. § 1031 (major fraud against the United States), 18 U.S.C. § 287 (false claims), conspiracy charges under 18 U.S.C. § 371, or wire fraud charges under 18 U.S.C. § 1343 —all common in government contract fraud prosecutions. See information about target letters and indictments. Target letters often include something that resembles opportunity : an invitation to meet with prosecutors, testify before the grand jury, or “present your side of the story.” This is where contractors without an experienced government contract fraud lawyer make irreversible mistakes. Anything you say to federal investigators—even in what feels like an informal conversation—becomes grand jury testimony and can be used against you in government contract fraud cases. Federal agents are trained to extract admissions, to get you to explain transactions in ways that can be reframed as knowing misrepresentations, to create statements that contradict other evidence in the government contract fraud case. Your government contract fraud lawyer will advise you: receiving a target letter doesn’t mean you must testify before the grand jury. You can decline the government’s invitation to meet. You can exercise Fifth Amendment rights. Your experienced government contract fraud lawyer will make strategic decisions about when, if ever, your testimony serves your interests in a government contract fraud investigation. In most cases involving government contract fraud allegations, it doesn’t. The DOJ’s criminal conviction rate is over 90%. Why is that? Most companies, government contractors and small businesses may choose to wait it out and take the chance on negotiating with federal agents and prosecutors in the hope that the problem goes away. It doesn’t go away once law enforcement sends you a target letter or civil investigative demand. Companies that achieve favorable outcomes recognize the value of a legal team that assesses, plans, and executes to increase their chances of better outcomes. Others consult a local criminal or commercial contract lawyer in hopes of making the problem go away. These are the main reasons the DOJ’s conviction rate is so high. Avoid Waiving Attorney-Client Privilege in Government Contract Fraud Cases When the government launches an investigation, it may often require meetings, issue a subpoena for documents or ask you to present yourself for questioning. One of the essential aspects of defending against any type of government contractor fraud case is to avoid waiving attorney-client privilege or waiving any constitutional right you have. You can very easily waive attorney-client privilege based on what you communicate to third parties. These are areas that our government contractor fraud attorneys can assist you with. The Real Questions Keeping You Awake at Night “Will I Face Prison Time?” The answer depends on multiple factors, but the statutory maximums are severe. For major fraud against the United States—the most serious government contract fraud charge—defense contractor fraud attorneys commonly see charges carrying up to 10 years imprisonment per offense. Conspiracy charges add another 5 years. Wire fraud charges involving government contracts carry 20-year maximums. If prosecutors charge you with multiple counts—separate false claims across different submission periods in government contract fraud cases—your potential sentence multiplies. But here’s what your government contract fraud lawyer will tell you: prison sentences aren’t inevitable, even when government contract fraud charges seem serious. Many experienced defense contractor fraud attorneys help their clients achieve resolutions avoiding incarceration entirely. Voluntary disclosures, early cooperation, and genuine remediation can result in civil-only resolutions or settlements with greatly reduced criminal exposure in government contract fraud matters. The contractors facing the harshest sentences are typically those who obstruct investigations into government contract fraud, destroy evidence, or continue fraudulent conduct after learning about the government’s scrutiny. Were you raided with a search warrant – was it valid? Find out more. Your skilled defense contractor fraud attorneys will analyze the government’s evidence rigorously in government contract fraud cases. Are there defenses to the charges? Are the alleged government contract fraud violations actually contract violations or regulatory misinterpretations? Did you have reasonable reliance on advice from counsel or the contracting officer? Could the falsity be disputed? Your experienced defense contractor fraud attorneys will identify these issues early and use them as leverage in settlement negotiations for government contract fraud defense. “Can My Company Survive This?” Federal government contract fraud investigations threaten your company’s existence in multiple ways beyond the immediate legal exposure. Beyond potential criminal fines and restitution, you face suspension and debarment—permanent or temporary exclusion from federal contracting. For companies deriving significant revenue from government work, debarment resulting from government contract fraud investigations is potentially terminal. You lose current contract revenue. You lose the ability to bid future work. The government contract fraud investigation itself damages your market position. Competitors learn about the scrutiny. Banks and bonding companies get nervous about your stability. Insurance carriers increase premiums or cancel coverage. Employees worry about their futures and accept offers from competitors. Even if you’re eventually cleared of government contract fraud allegations, the process often takes years and costs millions in legal fees. Yet companies do survive government contract fraud investigations . The ones that do typically have engaged experienced government contract fraud lawyer representation early, mounted a defense that addressed the government’s core concerns while protecting their interests, and demonstrated genuine commitment to compliance improvement. Your government contract fraud lawyer will help you navigate not just the legal proceedings, but the business realities of defending your contracts and your reputation during government contract fraud investigations. With law offices in Washington, DC, Los Angeles, Houston, Miami, and in Denver, Colorado , the defense contractor fraud attorneys at Watson & Associates are uniquely positioned to help small businesses and larger federal contractors throughout the United States to withstand the impact seen when criminal charges and civil fines are imposed. See information about avoiding criminal liability under PPP loan fraud cases . “What About My Employees?” Individual employees—especially executives, program managers, and those who signed certifications or prepared invoices—can face personal criminal liability in government contract fraud cases. Federal prosecutors frequently charge both the company and key individuals in government contract fraud prosecutions. The conspiracy provisions are particularly dangerous. Under federal law, you can be held criminally liable for the actions of co-conspirators even if you didn’t personally submit the government contract fraud false claim. Your government contract fraud lawyer must address these dynamics carefully. Company interests and individual employee interests may diverge in government contract fraud matters. An employee facing personal charges may be pressured to cooperate against the company or other employees in government contract fraud investigations. Your government contract fraud lawyer will ensure individuals’ constitutional rights are protected and that potential conflicts are addressed before they become irreparable. “How Much Will This Cost?” The financial reality is significant. Experienced defense contractor fraud attorneys typically charge $250 to $800 per hour. Straightforward government contract fraud cases might cost $125,000 to $200,000 in legal fees. Complex government contract fraud cases with multiple defendants, extensive document production, and potential trial reach $500,000 to several million dollars. Some defense contractor fraud attorneys work modified contingency or alternative fee arrangements for qui tam whistleblower government contract fraud cases. But the cost of not having experienced counsel is exponentially higher. Contractors who respond to CIDs without a government contract fraud lawyer, who provide voluntary statements to investigators, or who hire attorneys unfamiliar with federal procurement regulations end up paying far more through larger settlements, harsher penalties, and criminal convictions in government contract fraud matters that could have been avoided. Your early investment in qualified government contract fraud lawyer representation often reduces your total exposure by orders of magnitude. Common Government Contract Fraud Allegations and What Your Attorney Must Know Buy American Act Fraud and Trade Agreements Act Violations When your contract requires Buy American Act compliance, you’re certifying that products you deliver meet specific country-of-origin requirements. Providing foreign-made components while certifying domestic compliance is government contract fraud—even if those components are superior quality or less expensive. Defense contractor fraud attorneys see BAA and TAA violations prosecuted aggressively because they implicate national security and domestic manufacturing policy. Your government contract fraud lawyer must understand not just that a violation occurred, but whether legitimate exceptions or waivers applied to prevent government contract trade agreements act fraud , whether the contracting officer accepted foreign components despite requirements, or whether contract language was genuinely ambiguous about origin requirements. These are procurement-law defenses that distinguish a strong case from a vulnerable one in government contract fraud litigation. Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business SDVOSB Fraud SDVOSB fraud involves contractors fraudulently representing themselves as service-disabled veteran-owned to obtain set-aside contracts—a serious form of government contract fraud. Common schemes include “rent-a-vet” arrangements where a service-disabled veteran is the owner in name only. The SBA and VA investigate ownership structure, actual control over daily operations of the SDVOSB firm, and whether the veteran owner has requisite expertise in government contract fraud inquiries. Penalties are severe—conspiracy charges, wire fraud, and False Claims Act liability for every contract obtained through fraudulent certification in government contract fraud cases. Watson & Associates LLC’s SDVOSB government-contract fraud lawyer must determine whether actual control issues exist in your company or whether operational decisions were legitimately made by the veteran owner. Limitations on Subcontracting Violations Small business set-aside contracts require you to perform certain percentages of work yourself or with similarly situated small businesses—failure constitutes federal government contract fraud. For service contracts, you generally must perform at least 50% of contract costs with your own employees. Pass-through schemes are illegal forms of government contract fraud, but many contractors find themselves in gray areas about what constitutes adequate performance. Your government contract fraud lawyer will examine whether you legitimately needed specialized expertise, whether you truly managed subcontractors, what percentage of work your employees actually performed, and whether subcontractors were truly independent. These fact-intensive determinations require understanding both criminal law and limitations on subcontracting procurement regulations in government contract fraud defense. False Certifications and Defective Pricing Every invoice submission certifies accuracy and contract compliance—false certifications constitute government contract fraud. When you certify cybersecurity compliance you haven’t implemented, that’s government contract fraud. When you submit cost data and fail to disclose recent price decreases, that’s defective pricing government contract fraud. Recent enforcement actions indicate the DOJ is aggressively pursuing violations of cybersecurity certifications in government contract fraud investigations. Your government contract fraud lawyer must audit these certification systems and determine whether you had reasonable basis to make certifications, whether prior communications from the contracting officer created ambiguity about requirements, or whether compliance was technically infeasible in preventing government contract fraud allegations. Strategic Steps to Take Right Now Immediate Actions (Next 48 Hours): The moment you learn of a federal investigation into potential government contract fraud, do not panic—but do act immediately. Contact an experienced government contract fraud lawyer that same day. Not tomorrow. Not next week. Every hour matters because conversations continue to occur, documents continue to be created, and the government contract fraud investigation advances without your input. Stop all communication about the government contract fraud investigation except with your attorney. Do not discuss matters with employees, subcontractors, or family. Institute a document preservation hold immediately—instruct all employees to preserve documents and communications related to the contracts under investigation for government contract fraud purposes. Explicitly state that destroying documents is a federal crime. Do not speak with federal investigators about government contract fraud without your attorney present. Politely decline to answer questions. Say: “I want to cooperate fully, but I’d like to have my attorney present.” Then stop talking. Do not voluntarily produce documents until your government contract fraud lawyer has reviewed them for privilege, scope, and strategic considerations. DOJ, SBA and OIG Federal Investigations Lawyers Usually, when the contracting officer suspects fraud under the contract, he or she must refer the matter to the respective federal law enforcement agency. This could be the Inspector General (OIG) Office or some other federal agency. If you are involved in an SBA OIG or VA OIG investigation, give our federal investigations lawyers a call right away. See information about False Claims Act and defending those cases for contractors. Watson & Associates’ government contractor fraud lawyers also help clients during the initial investigation stages, suspension and debarment , through indictment in civil and federal criminal investigations, and all the way through trial. We help you to Avoid Costly Legal Mistakes Ultimately, government contractors can save significant time, money, and resources by taking the right approach and assembling the right legal team. When the government launches a procurement fraud investigation or criminal case for defense contractor fraud, it has unlimited resources. At Watson, our goal is to effectively plan for a defense that avoids common mistakes such as failure to disclose, not acting in good faith with federal agents, and not waiving rights or providing incriminating information to the federal government. The law firm represents clients from beginning to end. See information about hiring criminal defense attorneys for cases involving government contracts. What Not to Do Do not delete emails, destroy documents, or clean up files related to government contract fraud investigations. This is obstruction of justice—a separate federal crime with additional penalties. Do not contact witnesses or employees to “get your story straight” regarding government contract fraud allegations. Do not make statements on social media about the government contract fraud investigation. Assume investigators are monitoring your online presence. And absolutely do not assume you can talk your way out of government contract fraud charges by being honest and forthcoming without counsel. Why Your Choice of Government Contract Fraud Lawyer Matters Not all criminal defense attorneys understand federal contracting. Many excellent trial lawyers have never examined a FAR 52.219-14 limitation-on-subcontracting clause or understood how SBA program requirements create legal obligations relevan t to government contract fraud cases. When you hire a general criminal defense attorney for a government contract fraud matter, they’re learning procurement law while representing your company—and you’re paying premium rates for their education in government contract fraud litigation. Your government contract fraud lawyer should have actual experience working inside federal contracting. Former procurement officials, former DOJ prosecutors who specialized in government contract fraud defense, or former SBA attorneys bring an irreplaceable perspective. They understand how contracting officers think, what investigators prioritize, and what prosecutors know they can prove versus what remains uncertain in government contract fraud prosecutions. The best government contract fraud lawyer also understands that your goal isn’t a courtroom victory—it’s protecting your business, minimizing exposure, and enabling your company to continue operating. This means strategic CID responses in government contract fraud matters, careful evaluation of settlement versus litigation, and sometimes tough conversations about what government contract fraud cases should be resolved versus what cases can be won. Your Next Step If you’ve received a Civil Investigative Demand, a target letter, or learned that a federal government contract fraud investigation is underway, you’re facing decisions that will determine your company’s future. The time to engage an experienced government contract fraud lawyer is now—not after you’ve made statements to investigators, not after you’ve responded to CIDs, not after charges are filed. We help with CID responses, target letter negotiations, internal investigations, and aggressive courtroom defense when necessary. Our approach combines deep understanding of federal procurement law with sophisticated criminal defense strategy in government contract fraud matters. Contact us at 1.866.601.5518 for a confidential consultation about your government contract fraud case, and schedule a time to discuss your situation. Main Content: Tips on How to Prepare a Contract Disputes Act Claim Against the Government Skip to content If you are contemplating writing a contract claim to the federal government under the Contract Disputes Act you should consider the complexity and issues that can cause delays and cause the contracting officer to deny the claim. If you decide to prepare your own claim, consider the following as part of your approach. Ensure that your claim is in compliance with applicable laws and regulations. Determine the basis of the claim, including any violations or mistakes made by the government that you can use to support your claim. Make sure to include all necessary supporting documentation such as contracts, invoices, estimates, work orders and other relevant items that prove your case. Additionally, make sure to include any past and current communications with the government related to your claim and that you have addressed every point of contention raised by the government. Finally, ensure that all deadlines are met for submitting the claim. It is also important to remember that filing a contract claim requires patience and persistence as the process can often take several months. As a claimant, you should stay up to date with the progress of your claim and be prepared to field questions and requests from government representatives. You may have to provide additional evidence or submit additional information as it is requested. It’s important to remain proactive throughout the entire process in order to ensure that your claim is handled in an efficient Preparing a Contract Disputes Act (CDA) claim against the government requires careful attention to detail and adherence to specific procedures. Here’s some items to consider when preparing a CDA claim: Review the contract: Thoroughly examine the contract, including any amendments or modifications, to understand the rights and obligations of the parties involved. Identify relevant clauses related to claims, disputes, remedies, notice requirements, and dispute resolution mechanisms. Identify the claim basis: Determine the legal basis for the claim . Analyze the contract provisions, applicable laws and regulations, case law, and any relevant government policies or guidance. Ensure a clear understanding of the contractual breaches, damages incurred, and the causal connection between the government’s actions and the harm suffered. Evaluate notice requirements: Review the CDA and the contract to identify the specific notice requirements for filing a claim. Take note of the timeframe for providing written notice to the contracting officer and any other relevant parties. Verify compliance with all notice provisions. Gather supporting documentation: Collect all relevant documents and evidence to support the claim. This includes the contract, amendments, correspondence, change orders, invoices, receipts, project records, schedules, and any other records that substantiate the claim. Ensure that the documentation is organized, easily accessible, and comprehensive. Quantify damages: Accurately calculate and quantify the damages suffered by the contractor that is not contemplated or part of the original scope. Assess direct and indirect costs, additional expenses, lost profits, schedule impacts, and any other financial harm resulting from the government’s actions or failures. Support the calculations with detailed cost breakdowns and relevant financial records. Prepare a clear and concise claim statement: Draft a well-structured claim statement that clearly outlines the nature of the claim, the legal basis, the damages suffered, and the relief sought. Use plain language and avoid excessive technical jargon. Present the claim in a logical and persuasive manner. Address legal and factual defenses: Anticipate potential legal and factual defenses that the government may raise and address in the claim. Provide strong counterarguments supported by legal analysis, precedent, and factual evidence to refute potential defenses and strengthen the validity of the claim. Comply with procedural requirements: Ensure that the claim complies with all procedural requirements specified by the CDA, the contract, and any relevant agency regulations. This includes adhering to deadlines for submitting the claim, providing copies to all required parties, and following any additional procedural steps outlined in the contract or agency procedures. Engage in proactive communication: Maintain open and constructive communication with the contracting officer throughout the claim process. Respond promptly to any inquiries or requests for additional information. Seek clarification on any issues or concerns raised by the government and address them in a timely and professional manner. Seek expert legal advice if necessary: If the claim involves complex legal issues or substantial damages, consider engaging expert witnesses, consultants, or forensic accountants to provide specialized analysis and testimony. Consult with experienced procurement lawyers who have expertise in government contract law to ensure the claim is well-founded and effectively presented. Maintain a comprehensive claim file: Keep a well-organized claim file that includes all relevant documents, correspondence, and notes related to the claim. This file serves as a record of the claim process and can help track important deadlines, communications, and developments. Follow up and monitor progress: Regularly follow up on the claim’s status and monitor the progress of negotiations or any dispute resolution procedures. Maintain a proactive approach to ensure the claim is progressing and that any additional information or actions required are provided in a timely manner. Remember, the specific requirements and procedures for preparing a CDA claim may vary based on the jurisdiction and government agency involved. Consult with legal counsel experienced in government contract law to ensure compliance with all applicable rules and regulations. How Long Does the Contracting Officer Have to Give a Final Decision on a CDA Claim? Under the Contract Disputes Act (CDA) in the United States, the contracting officer has a specific timeframe to provide a final decision on a CDA claim. According to the CDA, the contracting officer is required to issue a written decision within 60 days from the receipt of the claim, unless they inform the contractor of the specific reasons for the delay and establish a reasonable time frame for issuing the decision. However, it’s important to note that this 60-day period can be extended by mutual agreement between the contractor and the contracting officer. In some cases, complex claims or disputes may require additional time for evaluation and analysis. If the contracting officer fails to issue a decision within the required timeframe or any agreed-upon extension, the contractor may be entitled to treat the absence of a decision as a deemed denial of the claim. This deemed denial allows the contractor to pursue further action, such as appealing the decision or initiating litigation. It’s crucial for contractors and their legal counsel to carefully monitor the timeline for the contracting officer’s decision and, if necessary, follow up with the contracting officer to ensure compliance with the required timeframe. Legal counsel can also provide guidance to contractors on how to proceed if the contracting officer fails to issue a decision within the specified timeframe. This is especially important where additional action may be required in order to protect the contractor’s rights and remedies. Contractors should always ensure that they are aware of their rights under government contracts and take appropriate measures when needed. What are the Steps for Appealing a Contracting Officer’s Final Decision When She Denies Your Claim? Appealing a Contracting Officer’s Final Decision is possible, but can be difficult. The first step is to carefully review the contracting officer’s decision and ensure that you understand the reasons for their denial of your claim. You will then need to decide if you want to pursue an appeal as there are specific requirements that must be met in order for an appeal to be successful. If you decide to pursue the appeal, you will need to submit a Notice of Appeal in writing within 30 days of receipt of the Contracting Officer’s Final Decision letter. The notice should include a statement that outlines why you believe the decision was incorrect and must also include any supporting documents. To appeal a Contracting Officer’s Final Decision when your claim has been denied, you typically need to follow a set of steps. While the specific process may vary depending on the jurisdiction and the agency involved, here is a general outline of the steps involved in appealing a contracting officer’s decision: 1. Review the decision: Carefully review the contracting officer’s final decision to understand the rationale behind the denial of your claim. Analyze the grounds cited, legal reasoning, and any factual findings made. Assess the strength of your case and the likelihood of success on appeal. 2. Determine the appropriate forum: Identify the appropriate forum for the appeal. In the United States, appeals are typically filed with either the agency board of contract appeals (such as the Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals or the Civilian Board of Contract Appeals) or the United States Court of Federal Claims. Consult the applicable regulations and seek legal advice to determine the appropriate forum for your specific case. 3. Know the filing deadlines: Be aware of the deadlines for filing an appeal. The timelines can vary depending on the forum. Generally, the appeal must be filed within a specified number of days from the date of the contracting officer’s final decision. Failure to meet the deadline may result in the appeal being dismissed. 4. Prepare the appeal: Prepare a comprehensive written appeal that presents a clear and concise argument against the contracting officer’s decision. Address the grounds for denial, providing legal and factual analysis to support your position. Cite relevant laws, regulations, contract provisions, and precedents to bolster your case. Include all necessary documentation and evidence to substantiate your arguments. 5. File the appeal: Submit the appeal to the appropriate forum within the prescribed timeframe. Follow the specific procedures and requirements of the forum, including any filing fees, formatting guidelines, and service of process requirements. Keep copies of all documents and proof of submission. 6. Engage in the appeal process: Once the appeal is filed, the process may involve various stages, such as a conference, discovery, motions, and potentially a hearing or trial. Familiarize yourself with the rules and procedures of the appeals forum and comply with all necessary requirements. Engage in the process actively, presenting your case effectively and responding to any opposing arguments or motions. 7. Consider settlement or alternative dispute resolution: Throughout the appeal process, there may be opportunities for settlement discussions or alternative dispute resolution methods, such as mediation or arbitration. Evaluate the feasibility of resolving the dispute through negotiation or alternative means and consult with legal counsel to determine the best course of action. 8. Await the decision: Once the appeal is fully briefed and the hearing or trial is complete, the appeals forum will issue a decision. Be prepared to wait for the decision, which can take some time depending on the complexity of the case and the caseload of the forum. The decision may affirm, reverse, or modify the contracting officer’s decision. 9. Assess further options: If you are dissatisfied with the outcome of the appeal, you may have further recourse. Depending on the circumstances, you may consider pursuing further appeals, seeking judicial review, or exploring settlement negotiations. Appealing a contracting officer’s decision can be a complex and challenging process. It’s crucial to consult with a Government Contract Disputes Act a ppeals attorney that is also experienced in government contract law to navigate the specific requirements and procedures of the appeals forum and increase your chances of a successful outcome. What is a Civil Investigative Demand? A Civil Investigative Demand (CID) is a powerful enforcement tool that allows federal agencies to compel individuals and businesses to produce documents, data, and testimony without first filing a lawsuit or obtaining court approval. Because a CID functions as an administrative subpoena, it gives the government broad access to information at a very early stage of an investigation. Failing to respond correctly—or on time—can expose a company or individual to significant legal and financial risk. Federal agencies, including the U.S. Department of Justice ( DOJ ), U.S. Attorney’s Offices, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), and the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), routinely issue CIDs as part of civil enforcement investigations. While these inquiries are technically “civil” in nature, they can carry serious consequences. Civil findings may result in substantial monetary penalties, and if investigators uncover evidence suggesting intent or knowing misconduct, the matter can quickly escalate into a criminal case. Unlike traditional subpoenas issued in litigation, a civil investigative demand does not require judicial oversight before it is served. Agencies have the authority to issue CIDs on their own initiative and often do so before any formal complaint is filed. The ability to challenge a CID is extremely limited, leaving recipients with broad and often burdensome compliance obligations. In many cases, companies are required to produce extensive volumes of documents and information while having little insight into the scope, theory, or direction of the government’s investigation. False Claims Act Civil Investigative Demand Attorneys:: CID Investigations FAQs CID Meaning: When does the CID investigation begin? The CID will send a Notice of Investigation to the subject of the inquiry. This notice will typically include instructions on how to respond, as well as a date by which all documents must be provided. It is important to provide any requested documents prior to this deadline in order to avoid potential penalties. Typically, the CID will review the documents and may request additional information or clarification, if necessary. The CID may also conduct interviews with those involved in providing the documents to ensure accuracy and completeness. Once all the necessary information has been gathered, a report detailing their findings will be issued. The subject of the inquiry can then respond to the report’s findings and may be required to take corrective actions if found non-compliant. Ultimately, a decision will be made as to whether or not an enforcement action is necessary. Are civil investigative demands confidential? Yes. Civil investigative demands are confidential and should not be shared with anyone outside of those involved in the inquiry without permission from the CID. Failure to observe these requirements can lead to potential penalties, so it is important to cooperate fully with the investigation and abide by all confidentiality regulations. What types of enforcement actions can be taken? Can you refuse to cooperate with a CID investigation? No . Federal law enforcement — including the Department of Justice (DOJ), FBI, and GSA Office of Inspector General (OIG) — has significantly increased oversight of Trade Agreements Act (TAA) sourcing on government contracts. When products are not TAA compliant, the consequences can be severe: contract termination, False Claims Act exposure, criminal referral, and executive liability. If you are a government contractor, subcontractor, reseller, or manufacturer selling through a GSA Schedule, understanding how to be TAA compliant isn’t just about avoiding audits — it’s about protecting your business, your contract revenue, and your leadership team. Maybe it started with an internal question about a supplier’s documentation of compliance with the Trade Agreements Act ( TAA ) . And because they didn’t see how a small sourcing error could escalate into contract loss, civil penalties, or even a criminal indictment case under the False Claims Act. The bottom line is that federal contractors, subcontractors, manufacturers, and medical equipment providers are now under great scrutiny. What Does “TAA Compliant” Really Mean? At its core, the Trade Agreements Act requires that products purchased by the federal government must be: Made in the United States, or Substantially transformed in a TAA‑designated country. Being TAA compliant means every product you offer under your GSA Schedule must satisfy one of those criteria for the entire duration of the contract — not just at award. This is a strict rule, and failing to meet it can trigger serious repercussions. Substantial Transformation — Not Just Assembly One of the leading sources of compliance risk involves misunderstanding what “substantial transformation” means. If a product is merely assembled in a TAA‑designated country from components sourced in non‑designated countries, it may not be TAA compliant. Federal auditors and the GAO have repeatedly stated that true transformation must result in a new product with a different name, character, or use. Simple assembly of imported parts often does not meet this standard. TAA‑Designated Countries The U.S. maintains an official list of countries whose products are considered compliant under the Trade Agreements Act. Products must be made or substantially transformed in one of these countries to be considered TAA compliant if not made in the U.S. However, many major manufacturing hubs — such as China and India — are not on the list. Knowing the origin of every component and subcomponent is essential to remaining compliant. A contract signed in good faith can quickly become a liability, especially if a whistleblower, auditor, or agency flags a product that appears to be out of compliance. To refelct on the reality of Trade Agreements Act fraud cases, the Department of Justice (DOJ) in September announced the unsealing of an indictment charging two Colorado companies and three executives with a multiyear scheme to sell Chinese‑made forklifts to the U.S. government while falsely representing that the equipment was manufactured in the U.S. and compliant with the Buy American Act (BAA) and the Trade Agreements Act (TAA). The charges span 27 counts, including conspiracy to commit wire fraud, wire fraud, making materially false statements, and conspiracy to defraud the United States. Why Federal Enforcement Has Intensified In recent years, federal enforcement of sourcing rules — especially TAA — has increased for several reasons: 1. Supply Chain Complexity Global supply chains are more complex than ever. Manufacturers may shift production lines or suppliers without notifying resellers. A product that was TAA compliant yesterday might not be tomorrow, yet many companies fail to detect these changes until they are audited. 2. False Claims Act Risk Misrepresenting product origin on federal contracts can rise to the level of False Claims Act violations. Under the False Claims Act, companies can be liable for treble damages plus per‑claim penalties if they knowingly (or recklessly) submit false certifications about product origin. DOJ investigations increasingly tie TAA sourcing misstatements to False Claims Act cases, particularly in high‑volume contract environments. 3. High‑Profile Investigations Recent federal cases demonstrate how serious TAA sourcing issues have become. In several matters over the past few years, DOJ and Customs enforcement have brought actions against companies and executives for misrepresenting origin, leading to significant settlements and, in some cases, criminal referrals. While every case is fact‑specific, the trend is clear: agencies are not treating TAA compliance as a minor administrative issue — they are treating it as a core integrity requirement. What is the Trade Agreements Act (TAA)? Critical Compliance Requirements for Federal Contractors and Manufacturers Understanding TAA compliance and the Trade Agreements Act ( TAA ) requires knowledge of international trade rules. TAA, established in 1979, promotes fair global trade. When using GSA or FSS schedules, following TAA is crucial, not just preferred. For CEOs, federal contractors, and manufacturers supplying products to the U.S. government, compliance with the Trade Agreements Act represents one of the most consequential—and frequently misunderstood—regulatory requirements in federal procurement. The stakes have never been higher. The Department of Justice has publicly reaffirmed its aggressive enforcement of TAA compliance violations through the False Claims Act , with recent criminal prosecutions and civil settlements reaching into the tens of millions of dollars. Companies face not only treble damages and penalties exceeding $28,000 per violation but also criminal prosecution, suspension, debarment, and permanent exclusion from the lucrative federal marketplace. What CEOs and Executives Must Know About Trade Agreements Act Requirements The Trade Agreements Act establishes stringent country-of-origin requirements for products sold to federal agencies under covered contracts, particularly those exceeding $182,000 for supply contracts. For government contractors holding GSA Schedule contracts or competing for Department of Defense, Veterans Affairs, and other federal agency procurement opportunities, TAA compliance is not merely a technical checkbox—it is a material contract requirement that agencies rely upon when making payment decisions. Products must either be manufactured or “ substantially transformed ” in the United States or in one of approximately 60 TAA-designated countries. This designation excludes major manufacturing centers including China, India, Malaysia, and most of Asia—countries where many Fortune 500 companies, healthcare providers, pharmaceutical manufacturers, and medical device producers maintain substantial portions of their supply chains. The substantial transformation test requires that at least 50% of a product’s manufacturing l ifecycle occur in a compliant country, fundamentally altering the product’s name, character, or use. For medical equipment manufacturers, this means programming software, primary component manufacturing, and sterilization processes must occur in designated countries. For technology contractors, assembly operations must be sufficiently complex and meaningful—not merely attaching components or repackaging. Common FAQs – Questions for Government Contractors and Manufacturers Here are some of the most common questions your compliance team might hear — drawn from searches, industry forums, and real‑world pain points: 1. “Can we still be investigated if our supplier gave us a country‑of‑origin letter?” Yes. A supplier’s certificate of origin is valuable documentation, but it does not insulate your company from scrutiny if the product is not actually TAA compliant . Federal investigators will examine the underlying manufacturing and supply chain records. 2. “Does assembly in a TAA country make the final product compliant?” Only if the assembly rises to substantial transformation, simply bolting parts together in a designated country may not be sufficient. Federal auditors and GAO consistently require evidence that the product’s character or use was fundamentally changed in the TAA country. 3. “What happens if we discover a product is no longer TAA compliant?” If the product is already offered on a GSA Schedule or sold to an agency, immediate corrective action is critical. That may include removing the item from the contract, notifying contracting officers, and preparing for potential audit follow‑up. Proactive remediation — done before an audit or investigation — can significantly mitigate risk. 4. “Can resellers rely on OEM certifications?” Resellers can rely on OEM documentation only if they independently verify it . Blind reliance on supplier documents, without internal validation, can expose your company to False Claims liability. 5. “What documentation should we maintain to demonstrate we are TAA compliant?” Best practices include: Detailed certificates of origin Manufacturing process documentation Supply chain and logistics records Internal compliance audits Risk assessments for every product offered under the GSA Schedule Real Risks for Contractors Right Now FCA enforcement remains aggressive. We serve as TAA compliance lawyers and attorneys for criminal defense of government contractors nationwide and overseas. What are GSA TAA Compliance Requirements? The following are some of the key requirements for GSA TAA compliance: 1. Substantial Transformation The concept of “ substantial transformation ” is critical in determining TAA compliance. It refers to the change that occurs when a product undergoes significant manufacturing or processing in a particular country. – A product must undergo a substantial transformation in a designated country to be considered TAA compliant. The country where the product’s final substantial transformation occurs determines its origin for TAA purposes, rather than the country of origin of its individual components or materials. 2. Certificate of Origin: Manufacturers and suppliers must provide a TAA Certificate of Origin to demonstrate TAA compliance. Note that for government contracting, it is the agency that decides compliance issues. – The Certificate of Origin typically includes information such as the manufacturer’s name, product details, country of origin, and a statement confirming compliance with the TAA. 5. Compliance Documentation: – GSA TAA contracts require manufacturers and suppliers to maintain proper documentation to support TAA compliance. 6. Verification and Reporting: – GSA contractors may be subject to verification by the GSA or other authorized entities to ensure TAA compliance. Contractors may need to provide documentation and evidence of compliance upon request. What are TAA Non-Compliance Penalties? Non-compliance with Trade Agreements Act (TAA) requirements can result in various penalties and consequences for businesses. The specific penalties for TAA non-compliance can vary depending on the nature and severity of the violation, the contracting agency involved, and other factors. Here are some potential penalties for TAA non-compliance: 1. Contract Termination: 2. Financial Penalties 3. Suspension or Debarment 4. False Claims Act Liability: Submitting false or fraudulent claims related to TAA compliance can trigger liability under the False Claims Act ( FCA ). 5. Reputational Damage: 6. Criminal Prosecution: In cases involving intentional or deliberate violations of TAA requirements, criminal charges may be brought against individuals or businesses. TAA Compliant Meaning – What are TAA Compliant Countries? The Trade Agreements Act (TAA) designates certain countries as TAA compliant, meaning that products originating from these countries are eligible for government procurement contracts. The list of TAA compliant countries includes the following: 1. United States: The TAA considers products manufactured or substantially transformed in the United States as compliant. 2. World Trade Organization (WTO) Government Procurement Agreement (GPA) Countries: The TAA recognizes the countries that are parties to the WTO GPA as compliant. These countries include: – European Union member states: Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Croatia, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Malta, Netherlands, Poland, Portugal, Romania, Slovakia, Slovenia, Spain, Sweden. – Other GPA countries: Australia, Canada, Hong Kong, Iceland, Israel, Japan, Liechtenstein, New Zealand, Norway, Singapore, South Korea, Switzerland, Taiwan. 3. Free Trade Agreement (FTA) Partner Countries: The TAA recognizes certain countries that have free trade agreements with the United States as compliant. These countries include: – NAFTA countries (prior to the USMCA): Canada and Mexico. – Chile, Costa Rica, Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Peru, and Singapore (FTA partner countries as designated by specific trade agreements). Trade Agreements Act criminal liability protection: Consulting with attorneys for criminal defense of contractors charged with government fraud or facing investigation in government contracting and TAA compliance can provide valuable guidance and ensure adherence to the specific requirements related to TAA compliant countries . What are the exceptions to the GSA Trade Agreement Act? The Trade Agreements Act (TAA) includes several exceptions that allow for the procurement of products from non-designated countries while still maintaining compliance. These exceptions recognize specific circumstances where non-compliant products can be used under certain conditions. Here are some key exceptions under GSA (General Services Administration) contracts: 1. Non-Availability Exception: – This exception applies when products from TAA-designated countries are not available in sufficient quantities, not available at a reasonable price, or do not meet the required quality standards. – The non-availability exception allows for the procurement of non-designated country products if certain documentation and justifications are provided. 2. Public Interest Exception: – The public interest exception allows for the procurement of non-designated country products when it is determined to be in the public interest of the United States. 3. Information Technology (IT) Exceptions: – There are specific exceptions for IT-related products and services. – One such exception is the “Commercial-Off-The-Shelf (COTS)” exception, which allows for the procurement of IT products that are sold in substantial quantities to the general public, regardless of their country of origin. – Additionally, certain IT products that are specifically excluded from TAA compliance requirements, such as telecommunications equipment and software, may be procured from non-designated countries. 4. De Minimis Exception: – The de minimis exception applies to products that contain a small amount or percentage of non-compliant components or materials. – Generally, if the total value of non-TAA-compliant components or materials in a product does not exceed a certain threshold (currently set at 50% of the cost of all components), the product may still be considered compliant. It’s important to note that the exceptions to the TAA can vary depending on the specific contract, agency, or procurement circumstance. Government Contractors should carefully review the contract requirements and consult with legal professionals experienced in government contracting to determine if any exceptions may apply to their specific situation. Appropriate documentation and justifications are typically required when invoking an exception to ensure compliance with the TAA regulations. Trade Agreements Act False Claims Act Attorneys: Defending Against Trade Agreement Act Fraud Allegations When federal prosecutors allege that contractors falsely certified TAA compliance to obtain government payments, they invoke the False Claims Act—the government’s most powerful civil fraud enforcement tool. Trade Agreements Act False Claims Act Attorneys defend clients against claims that can result in liability of three times the government’s damages plus mandatory penalties ranging from $14,308 to $28,619 for each false claim or statement submitted.​ The False Claims Act’s “knowing” standard extends beyond actual knowledge to encompass deliberate ignorance and reckless disregard of the truth. When manufacturers certify products as TAA compliant without conducting adequate supply chain due diligence, when contractors fail to remove non-compliant products after manufacturing locations shift to prohibited countries, or when companies consciously disregard supplier warnings about component origins, they create False Claims Act exposure.​ Qui tam whistleblower lawsuits pose particular risks. Relators—often competitors, disgruntled employees, or professional whistleblowers—can file sealed complaints and receive 15-30% of any government recovery. Recent whistleblower cases involving TAA violations have resulted in multi-million dollar settlements with substantial relator awards exceeding $4.5 million. In one notable case, professional whistleblower Brady Folliard has filed multiple TAA-related False Claims Act suits against technology vendors, demonstrating that individuals beyond typical whistleblower profiles actively monitor contractor compliance. Trade Agreements Act False Claims Act Attorneys employ sophisticated defense strategies addressing each element prosecutors must prove: knowledge, falsity, and materiality. Following the Supreme Court’s landmark decision in Escobar , materiality requires showing that alleged non-compliance influenced the government’s payment decision. Defense counsel can demonstrate that agencies expressed willingness to “work with” contractors to resolve compliance issues rather than rejecting claims—powerful evidence of immateriality. GSA’s 2006 newsletter stating it would collaborate with vendors on TAA compliance matters rather than outright rejecting claims has proven dispositive in dismissing cases. ​ ​ Implementing Robust TAA Compliance Programs: Proactive Strategies for Risk Mitigation Rather than waiting for investigation, CEOs and compliance officers should implement comprehensive TAA compliance programs incorporating these essential elements: Supply Chain Mapping and Assessment : Conduct detailed audits identifying every product’s country of origin, including components and sub-components. Document manufacturing processes to support substantial transformation analyses. ​ Supplier Management Protocols : Establish written agreements requiring suppliers to certify TAA compliance, provide advance notification of manufacturing location changes, and grant rights to audit compliance. ​ Documentation and Record-Keeping Systems : Maintain digital and physical files containing certificates of origin, substantial transformation analyses, supplier certifications, and CBP rulings. Implement systems tracking country-of-origin codes and ensuring accurate representation in GSA Advantage and other government platforms. ​ Employee Training Programs : Educate procurement staff on verifying country of origin, train sales teams on identifying TAA-compliant products, and prepare customer service for handling compliance inquiries. ​ Regular Internal Audits : Schedule periodic reviews of product origins, update documentation when manufacturing changes occur, and monitor changes to TAA country lists.​ Third-Party Audits : Engage external compliance experts to conduct independent audits identifying blind spots and improvement opportunities.​ Centralized Compliance Functions : Designate compliance officers with authority and resources to ensure TAA adherence across all business units. ​ Companies like Fortinet have implemented industry-leading programs including centralized labeling facilities where country of origin is checked and entered into enterprise resource planning systems, quality assurance teams separately inspecting each TAA product, consolidated distribution through federally-authorized distributors, and annual TAA training for operations personnel. ​ The Intersection of TAA Compliance and Other Federal Contracting Requirements TAA compliance cannot be addressed in isolation. Federal contractors must simultaneously comply with the Buy American Act, cybersecurity requirements including CMMC certification and DFARS provisions, E-Verify requirements, Section 889 restrictions on telecommunications equipment, and small business program regulations.​ The interplay between the Buy American Act and Trade Agreements Act proves particularly complex. While the TAA applies to contracts above specified thresholds and allows products from designated countries, the Buy American Act applies to contracts below TAA thresholds and imposes domestic content requirements. Small business set-aside contracts create additional complexity where non-manufacturer rule waivers may apply.​ Recent enforcement actions demonstrate DOJ’s willingness to pursue violations of multiple procurement requirements simultaneously. In fiscal year 2024, False Claims Act settlements exceeded $2.9 billion with 558 settlements and judgments, many involving procurement fraud allegations combining TAA violations with cybersecurity non-compliance, false cost and pricing data, and defective equipment certifications. ​ Trade Agreements Act Fraud & False Claims Act Defense: Protecting Your Company When Investigations Begin When federal investigators initiate Trade Agreements Act fraud defense proceedings, manufacturers and contractors face immediate decisions that will determine whether they face criminal prosecution, civil liability, or both. The Office of Inspector General and the Department of Justice have dramatically increased TAA compliance investigations, with record numbers of corporate executives facing criminal charges.​ Early engagement with experienced Trade Agreements Act fraud defense counsel proves critical for several reasons. First, anything company representatives say to investigators can be used in subsequent criminal or civil proceedings. Providing inconsistent or false statements during investigations can trigger additional criminal charges of making false statements under 18 USC § 1001 or obstruction of justice. Second, failure to preserve documentation—including contracts, purchase orders, invoices, certificates of origin, and communications with suppliers—can devastate defense strategies. Third, companies that self-disclose violations, fully cooperate with investigations, and implement robust remediation measures may receive substantial credit under DOJ’s False Claims Act disclosure policy, potentially capping damages at single rather than treble amounts.​ Defense strategies must address the government’s likely theories.
